dataset: Liverpool Cancer iChart Drug Interaction Database

pi_items.source: Mayo Clinic, FDA, Multiple peer-reviewed sources confirm Enzalutamide is an antiandrogen cancer drug used to treat prostate cancer (metastatic and non-metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer)
NCBI StatPearls, Mayo Clinic, UC San Diego Health - Warfarin is a vitamin K antagonist anticoagulant used to prevent blood clots

Multiple sources confirm this is a MAJOR drug interaction: (1) Drugs.com lists Enzalutamide with 265 major interactions total; (2) Enzalutamide reduces warfarin AUC by 56% (published studies); (3) Coadministration should be avoided or requires intensive INR monitoring; (4) Decreases anticoagulant effect, leading to thromboembolic risk (stroke, DVT, PE)

All keywords verified from authoritative sources: (1) CYP2C9 - Enzalutamide is documented as moderate CYP2C9 inducer and strong CYP3A4 inducer (NIH, Springer Nature Drug Safety); (2) inducer - Confirmed in multiple pharmacological references; (3) induction - Enzyme induction is the mechanism of interaction; (4) monitor - INR monitoring is explicitly recommended in all clinical literature (PubMed, clinical guidelines); (5) INR - International Normalized Ratio monitoring required due to warfarin interaction; (6) bleed - Risk of bleeding when warfarin levels rise after discontinuing enzalutamide as enzyme induction resolves
This is a task mechanics parameter (minimum screens to show in trajectory), not a medical/privileged information claim. Not subject to verification against medical data.

privileged_info_summary: Enzalutamide is an antiandrogen cancer drug used to treat prostate cancer. Warfarin is an anticoagulant. This is a major drug interaction: Enzalutamide is a strong CYP3A4 and moderate CYP2C9 enzyme inducer that increases the metabolism and clearance of warfarin, reducing its anticoagulant effect by approximately 56%. Clinical consequences include subtherapeutic INR and increased risk of thromboembolic events (stroke, deep vein thrombosis, pulmonary embolism). Coadministration should be avoided if possible, or if unavoidable, requires intensive INR monitoring and warfarin dosage adjustment (typically 30-50% increase needed). The interaction should be classified as RED (major/contraindicated) in the Liverpool Cancer iChart traffic light system. Key clinical features: CYP2C9 induction mechanism, enzyme induction effect, INR monitoring requirement, and bleeding risk if warfarin levels rise during discontinuation.
